one_line_takeaway: A QAML survey wrapped around a small simulated hybrid quantum-classical MNIST/EMNIST experiment showing that compounded white-box targeted (WTC) FGSM/PGD/CW attacks crush accuracy but adversarial training fully restores it, alongside a conceptual catalog of quantum-secure design principles.
key_finding: Adversarial training fully neutralized the compounded WTC attacks in the reported runs. On MNIST, clean accuracy ~100% dropped under attack to 16% (FGSM+CW), 5% (FGSM+PGD), and 92% (CW+PGD), and was restored to 100% with the adversarial-training defense. On EMNIST 'Digits', clean ~100%/98% dropped to 12% (FGSM+CW), 25% (FGSM+PGD), and 77% (CW+PGD), and recovered to 100% with defense. FGSM-containing compounds were far more damaging than CW+PGD, because (per the authors) FGSM is the most potent of the three and PGD already subsumes FGSM's principle, so FGSM+PGD adds little and CW+PGD (both small-perturbation L2/iterative) has the least impact. The paper concludes an HNN (and by extension a QNN) is as vulnerable as a classical CNN, but a suitable defense can fully mitigate the impact.
core_contribution: A survey-plus-empirical paper on Quantum Adversarial Machine Learning (QAML) with two distinct deliverables. (1) A conceptual contribution: three 'quantum-secure design principles' (use post-quantum cryptography; use quantum-resistant NN architectures; ensure transparent/accountable development and deployment), each mapped to quantum strategies (QDAI, QGAN/VQGAN, QEC, quantum encryption) and to security/privacy/trust design guidelines (Tables 6-12). (2) The paper's OWN novel empirical study: it builds a simulated hybrid quantum-classical neural network (HNN = classical CNN feeding a Cirq quantum circuit of ry rotation + CNOT entangling gates) and demonstrates a white-box, targeted, COMPOUNDED (WTC) adversarial attack -- combining two classical attacks at once -- against it, then shows adversarial training restores accuracy. The authors explicitly claim WTC (compounded) attacks against an HNN had 'not been studied yet.' The paper's stated aim is to argue that QNN models will be as vulnerable as HNN models and that defense strategies must be developed now.
threat_model: Adversary crafting adversarial examples against hybrid quantum-classical (and future quantum) neural networks. The paper frames a taxonomy: three adversary capability levels (lowest = noise/perturbation and measurement attacks; intermediate = quantum-gate manipulation and parameter tampering; highest = algorithm subversion and quantum cryptanalysis) and knowledge levels (white-, grey-, black-box). The empirical study's operative threat is specifically WHITE-BOX (full model knowledge), TARGETED (aims at a specific misclassification), and COMPOUNDED (perturbations coordinated across batches / two attacks combined) -- the WTC attack. Attack primitives used are the classical FGSM, PGD, and CW (Carlini-Wagner) methods, combined pairwise.
limitations: 1) No actual QNN and no real quantum hardware are tested -- the 'quantum' component is a small simulated Cirq circuit inside an HNN; the headline claim that 'a QNN model is just as vulnerable as an HNN model' is asserted, not measured on a QNN. 2) The defense recovers accuracy to exactly 100% on 5 of 6 cases, which is suspiciously clean and reflects evaluating adversarial training against the SAME attack it was trained on (no adaptive/unseen-attack evaluation); the paper's own future-work section admits adaptive and auto adversarial attacks were not studied. 3) The many proposed quantum defense strategies (QDAI, QGAN/VQGAN, QEC, quantum encryption) and the security/privacy/trust design guidelines are survey-level and entirely unevaluated. 4) Only MNIST/EMNIST digits (toy vision) are used. 5) The text is imprecise (e.g., refers to 'Fig. 5' when it means the table; 'membership interface' for 'membership inference'). CAUTION for graph ingestion: several eye-catching numbers in the prose belong to CITED work, NOT this paper's experiment -- the JPEG-compression CIFAR-10/GTSRB gains (28.97%->79.57%, 27.44%->82.71%, 41.00%->73.37%, 68.19%->91.70%) are from Das et al. 2017, and the evasion-attack drops of 21.52%/29.07% on CICIDS/TRAbID 2017 are also cited related work.

datasets_used: MNIST (full 60,000 train / 10,000 test; paper used a subset of 12,665 train / 2,115 test per Table 4)
EMNIST 'Digits' (full 240,000 train / 40,000 test; paper used 48,000 train / 8,000 test per Table 4)
quantitative_results: MNIST FGSM+CW: clean accuracy 100%, no-defense post-attack 16%, with-defense 100% (loss 0.000641 / 3.93 / 0.00759)
HNN hyperparameters: 4 layers, batch size 64, 10 epochs, learning rate 0.001, Adam, NLLLoss, ReLU + Log-Softmax
baselines_compared: No-defense HNN vs. adversarial-training-defended HNN (the paper's core comparison, per-attack)
Three compounded attack combinations compared against each other: FGSM+CW vs. FGSM+PGD vs. CW+PGD
Implicit HNN-vs-CNN framing (HNN treated as an extension of a classical CNN); no separate CNN or true-QNN accuracy numbers are reported as a baseline
